front.title: Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC

abstract.t: This document specifies multicast delivery mechanisms and catalog-based endpoint discovery for Media over QUIC (MoQ). It defines how MoQ sessions integrate with IP multicast (SSM, ASM), Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT), and TreeDN for scalable live streaming. The specification includes a multicast catalog extension for endpoint discovery, and delivery path selection across TV, mobile, and browser platforms. This document is container-format agnostic and is referenced by both and .

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Live streaming audiences routinely reach tens of millions of concurrent viewers. Traditional HTTP-based CDN architectures replicate streams at x86 server farms close to viewers, but this approach does not scale cost-effectively for 4K/8K/360-degree bitrates.

IP multicast provides an alternative where a single stream is replicated at the network layer, but lacks the economic incentives for widespread ISP deployment. Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides a modern transport for real-time media, but its unicast model faces the same scaling limitations as HTTP CDNs for mass-audience events.
This document specifies how MoQ integrates with IP multicast to combine the scalability of multicast with the reliability and signaling of QUIC:

ol.li: Delivery paths: Platform-specific multicast reception for TV, mobile, and browser clients
Catalog extension: A container-agnostic multicast endpoint discovery mechanism for MoQ catalogs

section.t: MoQ relays MAY operate as TreeDN nodes for hierarchical distribution.
This specification is container-format agnostic. It works with MMT , CMAF , LOC , or any other MoQ packaging format.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 .

dl.dd: Isolated Web App -- a Chrome packaging format that grants DirectSocket API access

section.name: Delivery Paths
section.t: MoQ content can reach receivers via multiple delivery paths depending on platform capabilities:

tr.td: SSM direct via OS multicast API

section.t: Both native SSM and AMT tunneling require UDP socket access. MoQ/WebTransport is the universal fallback and requires no special platform capabilities.
Broadcast receivers (ATSC 3.0, ARIB STD-B60) consume MMTP streams natively over RF tuner hardware. MoQ integration occurs at the gateway/head-end level via TreeDN and AMT / DRIAD .

section.name: Transport Hierarchy
section.t: Multiple transports MAY be available for a given stream. The catalog signals which transports are offered via the multicast endpoint format () and standard MoQ subscription. Applications select transports based on availability and local policy.

section.name: Multicast Catalog Extension
section.t: For tracks available via multicast, the MoQ catalog includes a top-level multicast field containing an endpoints array for endpoint discovery.

section.name: Multicast Endpoint Format
section.t: The multicast field is a catalog extension per Section 3.1. Parsers that do not support multicast MUST ignore it.
The multicast field contains an endpoints array listing one or more multicast groups. A single-endpoint deployment uses a one-element array:
Multi-group deployments (e.g., per-quality ABR tiers or separate audio/video groups) use multiple elements:
Endpoint field definitions:

ul.li: "ssm": Source-Specific Multicast (RFC 4607). This is the default when sourceAddress is present.

dl.dd: SSM source IP address per . Required for Source-Specific Multicast. If omitted, implies ASM.
Track names available on this endpoint. Track names correspond to the track identifiers used in the MoQ catalog renditions.
Aggregate bandwidth of this endpoint in bits per second. Subscribers SHOULD check available network capacity before joining high-bandwidth multicast groups.

Network delivery configuration describing how subscribers can reach the multicast stream when native IP multicast routing is not available. May appear on individual endpoints or at the top-level multicast object to apply to all endpoints. See .

section.t: Subscribers receiving a catalog with multicast endpoints SHOULD auto-connect to the multicast group when multicast APIs are available (IWA DirectSocket, native UDP sockets, AMT tunneling). This enables a seamless upgrade path: subscribers first connect via MoQ/QUIC to receive the catalog and media, then optionally switch to multicast for lower-latency, FEC-protected delivery.

During multicast join (which may take 1-3 seconds for IGMP/MLD), subscribers SHOULD continue receiving via MoQ/QUIC. Once multicast data arrives, subscribers switch to the multicast path. This dual-path startup avoids join latency gaps.

section.name: Network Source Types
section.t: The networkSource field describes how subscribers can reach the multicast stream when native IP multicast routing is not available. It appears at the multicast level (applying to all endpoints) or on individual endpoints.
Defined types:

section.name: AMT (Automatic Multicast Tunneling)

dl.dd: AMT relay address (IP or hostname). When present, subscribers SHOULD connect directly to this relay.

ul.li: "driad": DNS Reverse IP AMT Discovery per . Subscribers query the source IP's reverse DNS for AMT relay records. This is the RECOMMENDED discovery method.
"manual": Relay address is provided in the relay field.

section.t: Subscribers SHOULD attempt relay discovery in this order:

ol.li: Use DRIAD discovery on the SSM source IP if discovery is "driad" or omitted

section.t: Note: DRIAD requires the SSM source IP to have a reverse DNS delegation with TYPE260 records. Publishers using cloud-hosted sources that lack reverse DNS control SHOULD provide the relay field as a direct fallback.

section.name: ATSC 3.0 (Broadcast Television)

dl.dd: Physical Layer Pipe ID. Defaults to 0 (base PLP).
Service ID within the broadcast multiplex.

section.t: This type indicates the multicast stream is available via an ATSC 3.0 broadcast signal . Receivers with ATSC 3.0 tuner hardware (USB dongles, built-in tuners, or NextGen TV sets) can receive the stream directly over RF without Internet connectivity.
Note: These tuning parameters are intended for receivers with ATSC 3.0 tuner hardware. MoQ subscribers without tuner hardware ignore this networkSource type and use AMT or MoQ/QUIC instead.

section.name: Multiple Network Sources
section.t: When a stream is available via multiple delivery technologies, networkSource MAY be an array:
Subscribers select the highest-priority available source per the transport hierarchy defined in .

section.name: Multicast Packet Formats
section.t: Each MoQ packaging type defines its own multicast UDP wire format. The catalog's packaging field tells receivers which parser to use: MMTP packets per , LOC objects per , or condensed packets per Section 7.4.

section.name: Multicast Security
section.t: SSM inherently limits traffic to authorized sources via (S,G) filtering. For additional security:

ul.li: DTLS-SRTP: Encrypt media payloads when confidentiality is required over multicast
Source Authentication: Validate source IP at AMT relay; for SSM, receivers verify (S,G) membership
FEC Integrity: FEC decoder inconsistencies can detect injection attacks; receivers SHOULD verify decoded media structure
Replay Protection: Sequence numbers enable detection of replayed or reordered packets
AMT Relay Trust: For AMT, trust is delegated to the relay's authentication mechanisms per

Content Authentication: For content integrity across untrusted relay chains, publishers MAY include an Auth Tag MoQ extension header per on source track objects. The auth tag (e.g., ALTA) enables receivers to verify that media content has not been modified in transit, complementing SSM source address validation and QUIC hop-by-hop integrity. The authentication scheme and tag size are signaled in the catalog.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions. The multicast catalog extension () is a JSON schema extension to MoQ catalogs and does not require IANA registration.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
DNS Reverse IP Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT) Discovery
TreeDN: Tree-Based Content Delivery Network (CDN) for Live Streaming to Mass Audiences
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection

section.name: Author's Address
